channel.title: easy-baking-posts posts - RSS Feed
channel.description: Fresh easy-baking-posts posts and cooking inspiration

item.title: Decadent Chocolate Lava Cakes for Lovers: A Sweet Treat
item.description: These decadent chocolate lava cakes are the ultimate dessert for chocolate lovers. With a gooey center, they're perfect for date nights or special occasions!
item.dc:creator: author_001
item.content:encoded:
⏱️ Prep Time: 15 minutes

🍳 Cook Time: 12 minutes

⏰ Total Time: 27 minutes

🍽️ Servings: 4

📊 Difficulty: Medium

🥘 Ingredients

  • 1/2 cup unsalted butter
  • 1 cup semi-sweet chocolate chips
  • 2 large eggs
  • 2 large egg yolks
  • 1/4 cup granulated sugar
  • 2 tablespoons all-purpose flour
  • 1/4 teaspoon salt
  • Powdered sugar for dusting
  • Vanilla ice cream for serving

👩‍🍳 Instructions

  1. Preheat your oven to 425°F (220°C) and grease four ramekins with butter.
  2. In a microwave-safe bowl, melt the butter and chocolate chips together in 30-second intervals until smooth.
  3. In a separate bowl, whisk together the eggs, egg yolks, and sugar until thick and pale.
  4. Fold the melted chocolate mixture into the egg mixture, then gently stir in the flour and salt until just combined.
  5. Pour the batter evenly into the prepared ramekins and place them on a baking sheet.
  6. Bake for 12-14 minutes, until the edges are firm but the center is soft.
  7. Let them cool for 1 minute, then carefully invert onto plates.
  8. Dust with powdered sugar and serve with a scoop of vanilla ice cream.

📌 Pinterest Tags: #cat1761230294638 #Mediumpost #quickpost #30minutemeals #post #cooking #foodie #homemade #delicious #unsalted #semisweet #large

📌 Save this post to Pinterest!

media:content.media:title: Decadent Chocolate Lava Cakes for Lovers: A Sweet Treat
media:content.media:description: These decadent chocolate lava cakes are the ultimate dessert for chocolate lovers. With a gooey center, they're perfect for date nights or special occasions!

item.title: Southern Pecan Pie: Achieving the Perfect Custard-Set Filling
item.description: A properly balanced Southern pecan pie with a custard-like filling that sets cleanly without the gummy center or crystallized edges. Learn the correct egg-to-syrup ratio and cooling techniques that create the ideal texture, based on fifteen years of holiday baking.
item.dc:creator: author_001
item.content:encoded:
⏱️ Prep Time: 15 minutes

🍳 Cook Time: 50 minutes

⏰ Total Time: 65 minutes

🍽️ Servings: 8

📊 Difficulty: Medium

🥘 Ingredients

  • 1 unbaked 9-inch pie crust (store-bought or homemade)
  • 1 cup light corn syrup
  • 1 cup brown sugar, packed
  • 4 large eggs
  • 2 tablespoons unsalted butter, melted
  • 1 teaspoon pure vanilla extract
  • 1 cup pecan halves (about 4 ounces)
  • ¼ teaspoon fine sea salt

👩‍🍳 Instructions

  1. 1 Position the oven rack to the lower-middle position and preheat to 350°F (175°C). The lower-middle position puts the pie closer to the heat source at the bottom, ensuring the crust bakes through instead of staying pale and underbaked. This position keeps the pie away from side heating elements that can overbake the edges before the center sets. 10-15 minutes for preheating Oven reaches 350°F, indicated by preheat signal or thermometer
  2. 2 Remove the pie crust from packaging and unroll it gently into a 9-inch pie dish. Press it into the dish without stretching. Trim the overhang to about half an inch and crimp the edges decoratively if desired. Using a fork, poke the bottom of the crust in four or five scattered places—not frantically, just a few pricks that create tiny steam vents. This prevents the crust from puffing dramatically during baking without creating channels for filling to seep through. 3-5 minutes Crust is evenly fitted with no air pockets, edges are trimmed, bottom has 4-5 small fork pricks visible
  3. 3 In a large mixing bowl, use your fingers to break apart any clumps in the brown sugar, pressing them against the bowl. This step is crucial because grainy spots in the baked filling come from undissolved brown sugar. Once the sugar is completely broken down, add the corn syrup, eggs, melted butter, vanilla, and salt to the bowl. 2-3 minutes Brown sugar is completely free of lumps, all ingredients are in the bowl
  4. 4 Whisk the filling mixture steadily for 1-2 minutes until completely smooth. You're looking for a mixture with no visible brown sugar granules and uniform color throughout. You'll actually hear the difference as you whisk—it changes from a slightly grainy sound to a silky, smooth sound. This aeration also contributes slightly to a lighter, less dense final texture. 1-2 minutes Mixture is completely smooth with no visible granules, color is uniform throughout, whisking sounds silky rather than grainy
  5. 5 Stir in the pecan halves, distributing them evenly throughout the filling. Stirring them in now, rather than scattering them on the crust bottom, prevents them from forming a single pecan layer at the bottom. Instead, they'll be suspended throughout the filling, creating better texture distribution. 1 minute Pecans are scattered throughout the filling, none are clustered in one area
  6. 6 Pour the filling into the pie crust slowly and deliberately, distributing the pecans as you pour by occasionally stirring the bowl. Fill to approximately half an inch from the rim—filling to the very top causes it to seep out during baking and over-fill the dish. Before sliding the pie into the oven, make absolutely certain your oven rack is level. Even a slight tilt causes filling to pool on one side and seep underneath the crust. 2 minutes Filling reaches approximately half inch below rim, pecans are distributed throughout, crust is not disturbed, rack is visibly level
  7. 7 Carefully place the pie on the preheated oven rack at the lower-middle position. Close the oven door gently to avoid jostling the filling. Set a timer for 40 minutes. The pie will bake undisturbed for this initial period, during which the edges and filling will begin to set. 40 minutes Pie is safely on the rack, oven door is closed, timer is set for 40 minutes
  8. 8 When the timer reaches 40 minutes, perform the jiggle test. Open the oven door carefully and gently shake the pie dish with a side-to-side motion—not violently, just a simple gentle shake. You're evaluating the filling's movement. The outer edges should be completely set with zero jiggle. The center area, approximately 2 inches across, should have a barely perceptible wave, similar to Jello that's almost set but not quite finished. This carryover cooking stage is crucial. The residual heat will set that center perfectly as it cools, transforming it from liquid to proper custard. 40 minutes total cooking time Outer edges of filling show no movement when dish is shaken, center 2-inch area has slight wave (not dramatic wobble), crust edges are golden brown
  9. 9 If the entire filling jiggles like water, the pie needs more time. If nothing moves at all, you've likely reached the edge of overbaking and edges may develop graininess. If the jiggle test indicates more cooking is needed, bake for an additional 5-10 minutes and retest. The filling's internal temperature should reach approximately 170°F, though most home bakers assess doneness by the jiggle test rather than using a thermometer. 5-10 additional minutes if needed If edges are set and center has slight wave, proceed to step 10; if more cooking is needed, retest after 5 minutes
  10. 10 Once the pie passes the jiggle test, remove it from the oven using oven mitts. Place it on a wire cooling rack rather than directly on the countertop. The rack allows air to circulate underneath, promoting even cooling. Do not cover the pie. Leave it undisturbed at room temperature for at least 3 to 4 hours before slicing. This cooling time is non-negotiable. The filling transforms from liquid to proper custard as it cools, and slicing into a warm pie releases steam that prevents this critical transformation. I learned this the hard way after slicing into pies after only 2 hours and finding the filling was still too soft. 3-4 hours Pie cools at room temperature, surface gradually becomes more opaque, pie reaches room temperature after 3+ hours
  11. 11 After 3 to 4 hours of cooling, the pie is ready to slice and serve. For the cleanest cuts, use a long, thin knife dipped in hot water and dried between cuts. This prevents the filling from sticking to the blade and dragging through the pie, which tears the structure and makes slices look messy. A damp cloth helps clean the blade quickly between cuts. The filling should now be firm enough to slice while maintaining its tender, custard-like texture. 0 minutes (serving time) Pie is completely cooled to room temperature, filling is firm enough to slice cleanly, slices maintain their shape

📌 Pinterest Tags: #cat1761230294638 #Mediumpost #post #cooking #foodie #homemade #delicious #unbaked #light #brown

📌 Save this post to Pinterest!

media:content.media:title: Southern Pecan Pie: Achieving the Perfect Custard-Set Filling
media:content.media:description: A properly balanced Southern pecan pie with a custard-like filling that sets cleanly without the gummy center or crystallized edges. Learn the correct egg-to-syrup ratio and cooling techniques that create the ideal texture, based on fifteen years of holiday baking.

media:group.media:keywords: cat1761230294638, Mediumpost, post, cooking, foodie, homemade, delicious, unbaked, light, brown

item.title: Banana Bread: Mastering Moisture Balance and Crumb Structure
item.description: A guide to baking banana bread that achieves the ideal balance between moisture and structure. Learn why banana ripeness fundamentally changes the bread's outcome, how mixing technique directly affects crumb development, and the chemistry behind achieving a tender but structurally sound loaf that stays moist for days.
item.dc:creator: author_001
item.content:encoded:
⏱️ Prep Time: 15 minutes

🍳 Cook Time: 55 minutes

⏰ Total Time: 70 minutes

🍽️ Servings: 8

📊 Difficulty: Easy

🥘 Ingredients

  • 3 large ripe bananas (approximately 1.25 lbs / 570g), peeled and mashed
  • 1/3 cup (80ml) unsalted butter, melted
  • 1 tsp (5g) baking soda
  • 1/4 tsp (1.5g) fine sea salt
  • 3/4 cup (150g) packed brown sugar
  • 1 large egg (approximately 50g), beaten
  • 1 tsp (5ml) pure vanilla extract
  • 1.5 cups (190g) all-purpose flour
  • Optional: 1/2 cup (90g) chocolate chips or chopped walnuts

👩‍🍳 Instructions

  1. 1 Preheat your oven to 350°F (175°C) and position a rack to the middle of the oven. Prepare a 4x8 inch (10x20 cm) loaf pan by lightly coating the bottom and sides with butter or non-stick spray. If desired, line the bottom with parchment paper for easy removal. The middle rack position ensures even heat exposure—heat radiates from both above and below, which creates more even browning than if the loaf were positioned higher or lower. If your oven tends to brown items unevenly (many home ovens have hot spots), position the rack slightly lower to prevent the top from browning excessively before the interior cooks through. 10 minutes Oven reaches 350°F, steam comes out when door opens, rack is positioned in middle of oven
  2. 2 Select bananas that are very ripe, with significant brown spotting across their yellow skin—ideally covering 50-75% of the peel. At this ripeness stage, the banana's starches have been enzymatically converted to simple sugars, which dissolve throughout the batter. These bananas feel softer when squeezed and smell intensely banana-sweet. If your bananas are mostly yellow with minimal spotting, they're not ripe enough and will produce noticeably less sweet bread with a denser texture. Peel the bananas and place them in a mixing bowl. Using a fork, mash them thoroughly, pressing firmly to break down the soft flesh. Aim for mostly smooth consistency with a few small banana pieces remaining—these pieces create pleasant textural variation in the finished bread. You should have approximately 1 cup (240ml) of mashed banana, which is roughly 240g by weight. 3-4 minutes Bananas are mostly smooth with a few small pieces visible, mashed banana fills approximately 1 cup measure, color is uniform pale yellow
  3. 3 Pour the melted butter into the mashed bananas and stir to combine thoroughly. Use a spoon to mix for approximately 1 minute, pressing the banana mixture against the side of the bowl and stirring from the bottom. The warm butter—currently around 130-140°F (54-60°C) depending on how recently it was melted—slightly warms the bananas without cooking them. This step matters because it helps the egg incorporate more smoothly in the next step. Continue stirring until the butter and bananas are evenly mixed with no visible streaks of separate butter. 2 minutes Butter and bananas fully combined, no visible butter streaks, mixture is uniform pale yellow, no lumps of banana remain larger than 1/4 inch
  4. 4 Add the baking soda and salt to the banana-butter mixture and stir gently for about 20-30 seconds. You'll notice the mixture will slightly darken in color and develop small bubbles breaking the surface—this is the sodium bicarbonate reacting with the banana's malic and citric acids. The reaction produces carbon dioxide gas (which creates the bubbles you see), water, and sodium salts. This reaction happens immediately and primarily in the bowl before baking, though additional CO2 generation continues when heat is applied in the oven. Continue stirring only until you don't see any dry powder remaining. Overstirring at this stage develops gluten, which you want to minimize. 1 minute total Baking soda is fully incorporated with no visible powder, mixture has darkened slightly, small bubbles visible on surface, no dry white specks of baking soda remain
  5. 5 Add the brown sugar, beaten egg, and vanilla extract to the banana mixture. Beat the egg first in a separate small bowl with a fork until the white and yolk are completely combined and uniform in color. This pre-beating ensures the egg incorporates smoothly without creating visible streaks. Pour the beaten egg into the banana mixture and stir these ingredients together until just combined, approximately 30-45 seconds of stirring. You should see the egg integrate into the mixture as a uniform liquid, and the brown sugar should dissolve and distribute evenly. The mixture will appear lighter and more cohesive once the egg is fully combined. At this point, assess the batter's consistency visually—it should be thick and pourable, like cake batter rather than soup or dough. 2-3 minutes including egg beating Egg is fully incorporated with no visible streaks, brown sugar is dissolved and distributed, mixture is uniform in color and slightly lighter than the banana mixture alone
  6. 6 Add the flour in two additions to minimize gluten development. Measure the flour by spooning it into your measuring cup and leveling with a knife—do not scoop directly from the bag, which compacts the flour. First, add three-quarters of the flour (about 1 cup / 125g) and fold it in gently using a rubber spatula or wooden spoon, using 15-20 gentle strokes just until you no longer see dry flour streaks. The batter will look lumpy and rough, which is exactly what you want. Then add the remaining flour and fold gently for another 10-15 strokes until just combined. Stop mixing immediately as soon as you see no visible dry flour—there will still be visible lumps of flour throughout the batter, and this is essential. These small, incompletely hydrated flour lumps form tiny pockets in the final crumb as they absorb moisture during baking. Fully mixing the flour would develop gluten, which would create a denser, tougher crumb structure. This is the single most important technique step in banana bread baking. 2-3 minutes No visible dry flour streaks, but small lumps (about pea-sized or smaller) remain throughout batter, batter is thick and chunky, color is uniform
  7. 7 If using chocolate chips or chopped walnuts, fold them gently into the batter with just 2-3 additional strokes of the spatula. Do not stir the overall batter further after adding the mix-ins. The goal is to distribute the add-ins throughout without over-working the batter and developing more gluten. Limit add-ins to a maximum of 1/2 cup total by volume. If using chocolate chips, ensure they're completely dry before adding them. If using nuts, toast them in a dry pan over medium heat for 3-4 minutes until fragrant, then cool completely—this drives off surface moisture and concentrates their flavor. 1 minute Chocolate chips or nuts are visible throughout batter, distributed evenly without excessive stirring, overall batter consistency remains lumpy
  8. 8 Pour the batter into the prepared loaf pan and smooth the top gently with a spatula, pressing lightly to even out the surface without compacting the batter. The surface should be relatively level so the bread bakes evenly and doesn't develop a dramatic peak or dome in the center. Place the pan in the preheated oven on the middle rack. The batter will begin rising immediately as the oven's heat activates the baking soda and generates CO2. 2 minutes Batter is in pan with relatively smooth, even top surface, pan is on middle oven rack at 350°F
  9. 9 Bake for 50-60 minutes depending on your oven's specific characteristics and the thickness of the loaf. Begin checking for doneness at 50 minutes by inserting a wooden toothpick into the center of the loaf, roughly 1 inch from the edge if possible (the very center often bakes last). The toothpick should come out clean or with just a few moist crumbs clinging to it—not wet batter, which indicates underbaking, but a few crumbs are acceptable. At the same time, observe the bread's color. The top should be deep golden-brown, and the edges should be slightly darker and pulling away from the pan sides. If the top is browning too quickly but the toothpick still comes out wet with batter (indicating the interior isn't cooked through), tent the top loosely with aluminum foil for the remaining baking time. This prevents the exterior from burning while the interior finishes cooking. If you have an instant-read thermometer, the internal center should reach approximately 205-210°F (96-99°C). 50-60 minutes Top is deep golden-brown, edges are darker and pulling from pan sides, toothpick inserted in center comes out clean or with a few moist crumbs (not wet batter), internal temperature reaches 205-210°F if using a thermometer
  10. 10 Remove the banana bread from the oven and let it cool in the pan for 10-15 minutes. This cooling period is crucial—it allows the crumb structure to set and firm up enough to handle without falling apart. During this resting period, the internal temperature continues to rise slightly from residual heat, and the protein-starch network solidifies. After 10-15 minutes, place a wire cooling rack on top of the pan and carefully invert both together, allowing the bread to slide out onto the rack. The bread should come away from the pan sides easily at this point. If it seems stuck, run a thin knife around the inside edges before inverting. 15 minutes Bread has cooled enough that it comes away from pan edges easily, can be inverted without falling apart, is still warm to the touch
  11. 11 Allow the banana bread to cool completely on the wire rack, approximately 1-2 hours, before slicing. During cooling, the interior continues to set and firm up as moisture redistributes and the starches fully gelatinize. The bread will be warm after 30 minutes but still too soft to slice cleanly—slicing warm bread causes the crumb to compress and creates a denser texture in the slice than the bread actually has. If you must eat it sooner, allow at least 30 minutes before slicing, but the final texture will be noticeably better if you wait the full 1-2 hours. Once completely cool, the bread will slice cleanly, and the crumb should be visibly tender with fine, even texture. 60-120 minutes Bread is cool to the touch, internal temperature has dropped to room temperature, slices cleanly with a serrated knife without crushing the crumb

📌 Pinterest Tags: #cat1761230294638 #Easypost #post #cooking #foodie #homemade #delicious #large #unsalted #baking

📌 Save this post to Pinterest!

Perfect for meal planning and sharing with friends

media:content.media:title: Banana Bread: Mastering Moisture Balance and Crumb Structure
media:content.media:description: A guide to baking banana bread that achieves the ideal balance between moisture and structure. Learn why banana ripeness fundamentally changes the bread's outcome, how mixing technique directly affects crumb development, and the chemistry behind achieving a tender but structurally sound loaf that stays moist for days.

media:group.media:keywords: cat1761230294638, Easypost, post, cooking, foodie, homemade, delicious, large, unsalted, baking

item.title: Creamy Garlic Mashed Potatoes: Building Texture and Emulsion
item.description: A guide to making mashed potatoes that achieve creamy texture and balanced garlic flavor through proper emulsification and heat management. Learn why Yukon gold potatoes work better than russets, how starch and fat interact, and how to prevent gluey, broken texture.
item.dc:creator: author_001
item.content:encoded:
⏱️ Prep Time: 15 minutes

🍳 Cook Time: 20 minutes

⏰ Total Time: 35 minutes

🍽️ Servings: 4

📊 Difficulty: Easy

🥘 Ingredients

  • 2 pounds (900g) Yukon gold potatoes, peeled and quartered
  • 1/2 teaspoon (3g) fine sea salt, for boiling water
  • 6 tablespoons (85g) unsalted butter, divided
  • 4 cloves (20g) garlic, minced
  • 1/2 cup (120ml) heavy cream, warmed to room temperature
  • 1/4 teaspoon (0.5g) white pepper, or black pepper to taste
  • 1/4 teaspoon (0.5g) fine sea salt, for seasoning (adjust to taste)
  • Optional garnish: 1 tablespoon (3g) fresh chives, finely chopped

👩‍🍳 Instructions

  1. 1 Fill a large pot with approximately 2 quarts (1.9 liters) of cold water and add 1/2 teaspoon (3g) of salt. The salt should dissolve completely. Bring the water to a rolling boil, which takes approximately 8-10 minutes with a covered pot. While waiting for the water to boil, peel the Yukon gold potatoes using a vegetable peeler or paring knife. Cut them into quarters that are roughly equal in size, approximately 2-3 inches (5-7 cm) across. Uniform sizing ensures even cooking—smaller pieces will become mushy while larger pieces remain undercooked if sizes vary too much. 8-10 minutes for water to boil, 5 minutes for preparation Water reaches rolling boil with large bubbles bursting aggressively at the surface, potatoes are cut into uniform quarter-sized pieces
  2. 2 Once the water reaches a rolling boil, carefully add all the potato pieces. The water will temporarily drop in temperature as the cold potatoes are added. Maintain a gentle boil (not a rolling boil) by adjusting the heat to medium-high. The potatoes will be fully submerged as they sink. Set a timer for 15-18 minutes. The exact time depends on the potatoes' size and your stove's heat, but do not cook longer than 18 minutes—overcooked potatoes absorb excess water and become gluey when mashed. 15-18 minutes Potatoes are fork-tender and offer zero resistance when pierced with a knife, potato skin begins to visibly separate in places
  3. 3 While the potatoes cook, prepare the garlic and butter. Mince 4 cloves of garlic into small, uniform pieces approximately 1/8 inch (3mm) in size. Place a small saucepan over low heat and add 2 tablespoons (28g) of butter. Allow the butter to melt slowly without browning. Once melted, add the minced garlic and stir constantly for 1-2 minutes. The garlic should release its aroma and become slightly softened but never brown or crispy. Brown garlic tastes acrid and bitter. Remove from heat and set aside. Do not combine the garlic butter mixture with the remaining butter—keep it separate. 3-4 minutes Butter is fully melted and foamy, garlic is fragrant with no browning visible, mixture is golden and clear
  4. 4 Once the potatoes are fork-tender, immediately drain them in a colander but do not rinse them. Rinsing removes starch from the surface, which is essential for proper mashing texture. Reserve approximately 1/4 cup (60ml) of the cooking water in case the final mash needs thinning. Return the hot, drained potatoes to the warm pot. The residual heat will continue softening the potatoes slightly and will help the butter and cream integrate better. 2-3 minutes Potatoes are in the pot, steam is rising, pot is warm to the touch
  5. 5 Before adding any butter or cream, allow the potatoes to rest for 1-2 minutes to release some of their steam. This prevents excess moisture from creating gluey texture. Then, begin mashing using a potato masher, working from the bottom of the pot upward. Use steady, firm pressure rather than aggressive pounding. Mash for approximately 2-3 minutes until potatoes are mostly broken down but still contain some small lumps. You should not see large chunks, but a few small specks of potato (approximately the size of peas) are fine and contribute to texture. 3-4 minutes Potatoes are mostly smooth with texture like coarse sand, no large chunks remain, steam is rising steadily
  6. 6 Add the remaining 4 tablespoons (56g) of unsalted butter in two additions, stirring well after each addition. The butter should integrate quickly and create a glossy sheen. Then add the garlic-infused butter mixture and stir thoroughly. The heat from the potatoes will warm everything to the same temperature, promoting proper emulsification. 1-2 minutes Butter is fully integrated and creates glossy appearance, no visible butter streaks remain, mixture is cohesive
  7. 7 Pour the room-temperature cream into the pot in a thin stream while stirring constantly. Do not add all the cream at once. The steady stirring and gradual addition allow the cream to emulsify properly with the starch and fat. The potatoes will immediately become creamier and smoother as the cream incorporates. Continue stirring for 1-2 minutes after all cream is added until the texture is completely uniform and smooth. 2-3 minutes Cream is fully incorporated, texture is uniform and creamy throughout, no streaks of cream or butter visible
  8. 8 Taste the potatoes and season with white pepper (which is milder and less visible than black pepper) and additional salt as needed. Start with 1/4 teaspoon (0.5g) of salt and add incrementally, tasting after each addition. The potatoes should taste seasoned but not salty. The garlic should be apparent as a savory depth, not a sharp, biting flavor. If the mixture seems too thick, thin it with reserved potato cooking water, adding 1-2 tablespoons (15-30ml) at a time. 2-3 minutes Potatoes taste properly seasoned, flavors are well-developed and balanced, texture is creamy but not overly thick
  9. 9 Transfer to a serving dish immediately and garnish with chopped fresh chives if desired. Mashed potatoes are best served hot, within 15 minutes of finishing. If holding for longer periods, transfer to a warm oven (200°F / 93°C) and stir occasionally, but this changes the texture slightly over time as the fat separates. 5 minutes Potatoes are in serving dish, steam is rising, dish is warm to the touch

📌 Pinterest Tags: #cat1761230294638 #Easypost #1hourmeals #post #cooking #foodie #homemade #delicious #yukon #teaspoon #unsalted

📌 Save this post to Pinterest!

media:content.media:title: Creamy Garlic Mashed Potatoes: Building Texture and Emulsion
media:content.media:description: A guide to making mashed potatoes that achieve creamy texture and balanced garlic flavor through proper emulsification and heat management. Learn why Yukon gold potatoes work better than russets, how starch and fat interact, and how to prevent gluey, broken texture.

media:group.media:keywords: cat1761230294638, Easypost, 1hourmeals, post, cooking, foodie, homemade, delicious, yukon, teaspoon, unsalted
